questions.text: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity v and angular velocity ω is always
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 0 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.
When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 180 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.
When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is not 360 degrees. The correct angle is 90 degrees.

Correct! When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector and the angular velocity vector is always 90 degrees. This relationship is due to the perpendicular nature of these vectors in circular motion.

questions.explanation: When a body moves in a circle, the angle between its velocity vector (v) and angular velocity vector (ω) is always 90 degrees, or π/2 radians. This is because the velocity vector is tangential to the circle at any point, pointing in the direction of motion, while the angular velocity vector is perpendicular to the plane of the circle and points along the axis of rotation. As a result, the angle between them is always a right angle. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because the angle between velocity and angular velocity in circular motion is not 0, 180, or 360 degrees. The correct angle is always 90 degrees due to the specific geometric relationship between these vectors in circular motion.

questions.text: A body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle. Magnitude of angular acceleration is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
This option is correct.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below. If a body moves with constant angular velocity on a circle, then its angular acceleration is zero. This is because acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time. In circular motion, the velocity of the body is constantly changing as it moves around the circle, but the magnitude of the velocity remains constant. Therefore, the angular acceleration of the body is zero. Mathematically, we can express this as: Angular acceleration (α) = dω/dt where ω is the angular velocity of the body. Since the angular velocity is constant, the derivative of ω with respect to time is zero, so the angular acceleration is also zero.

questions.text: Work done due to centripetal force for circular motion will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Reduced: The work done is a constant value of zero, not a reduced amount of work.
The value of work done is zero, not half of any value.
The work done is minimum (zero), not maximum. Maximum work would occur if the force and displacement were in the same direction.

The work done by the centripetal force in circular motion is not "reduced" because the centripetal force doesn't result in work in the first place. This is because the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the direction of motion, and work is defined as the product of force and displacement in the direction of the force. When you calculate the work done, you need to consider the component of the force that is in the direction of motion (parallel to the displacement). In the case of the centripetal force, there is no component of the force in the direction of motion, as it always acts perpendicular to the displacement. In mathematical terms, when you calculate work as W = F * d * cos(θ), the θ (angle) between the force and displacement vectors is 90 degrees (perpendicular), and the cosine of 90 degrees is 0. Therefore, the work done by the centripetal force is zero, not half or any other fraction. So, in the context of circular motion, the centripetal force does not perform any work on the object because the force and the direction of motion are always perpendicular.

questions.explanation: The work done due to the centripetal force in circular motion is zero. This is because the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the displacement of the object in its circular path. In other words, the force is always directed toward the centre of the circle, while the displacement is along the circumference of the circle.The work done (W) is defined as the dot product of force (F) and displacement (d):W = F * d * cos(θ)Where:W is the work done.F is the force applied.d is the displacement.θ is the angle between the force and the displacement vectors.In the case of centripetal force in circular motion, the angle θ between the force and displacement vectors is 90 degrees (perpendicular), so the cosine of 90 degrees is 0. Therefore, the work done is:W = F * d * cos(90) = F * d * 0 = 0This means that no work is done by the centripetal force in circular motion because the force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion.

questions.text: The mud files off the tyre of a fast moving car in the direction
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Mud flies off the tyre of a fast-moving car is not parallel to the moving tyre in the direction of the tyre's rotation. When the tyre rotates, it creates a centrifugal force that pushes the mud outward. The mud is also moving forward with the car, so it has a forward velocity. The combination of these two forces causes the mud to fly off the tyre in a tangent to the tyre's rotation.

The mud flies off the tyre of a fast-moving car in the tangential direction to the moving tyre. This is because the mud is not moving in the opposite direction to the tyre. The mud is stationary relative to the ground, and the tyre is moving past the mud. The mud is held in place on the tyre by friction. However, the frictional force is not strong enough to keep the mud on the tyre when the tyre is moving very quickly. The mud is eventually flung off the tyre in the tangential direction.

The mud flies off the tyre of a fast-moving car tangentially to the moving tyre in the direction of the car's motion. This is because the mud is attached to the tyre and is moving with the tyre at the same speed. When the tyre hits a bump, the mud is momentarily slowed down, but the tyre continues to move forward. This causes the mud to fly off in the direction of the car's motion. The mud is also affected by the centrifugal force, which is a force that pushes objects outward from the centre of rotation. In this case, the centre of rotation is the axle of the tyre. The centrifugal force causes the mud to fly off in a tangential direction, but the force is not strong enough to overcome the force of inertia, which is the force that keeps the mud moving with the tyre.
Option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Points on a rotating tyre have an instantaneous linear velocity that is tangential to the circular path at that point. When mud stuck on the tyre loses contact, the centripetal force keeping it in circular motion disappears. The mud then continues moving in the direction of its instantaneous velocity at the point of separation. Since this velocity is tangential to the tyre, the mud flies off along the tangent.

questions.text: A cyclist turns around a curve at 15miles/hour. If he turns at double the speed, the tendency to overturn is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If a cyclist turns around a curve at double the speed, the tendency to overturn is quadrupled. The centripetal force is directly related to the speed squared in the equation F_c = mv²/r, leading to this quadrupling effect.
If the cyclist turns at double the speed, the centripetal force required to keep the cyclist in a circular path actually increases, leading to the tendency to overturn being quadrupled, not halved.

Turning at double the speed affects the centripetal force, which is directly proportional to the speed squared. This means that the tendency to overturn is not unchanged but actually quadrupled in this scenario.

While it may seem intuitive that doubling the speed would double the tendency to overturn, in circular motion, the relationship between speed and the centripetal force is squared. Thus, doubling the speed actually leads to the tendency to overturn being quadrupled.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. If a cyclist turns around a curve at double the speed, the tendency to overturn is quadrupled due to the relationship between speed and the centripetal force. The centripetal force is crucial in keeping the cyclist in a circular path, and when the speed is doubled, this force quadruples. Therefore, the correct answer is quadrupled. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they do not consider the squared relationship between speed and centripetal force in circular motion.

questions.text: A car is moving with high velocity when it has a turn. A force acts on it outwardly because of
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: No, the force that acts on a car outwardly when it makes a turn is not centripetal force. Centripetal force is a force that acts toward the centre of a circle, and it is what keeps the car moving in a circular path. The force that acts outwardly on the car is called centrifugal force.

No, a force does not act on a car outwardly because of the gravitational force when it makes a turn. The force that acts on the car outward is called the centrifugal force. The centrifugal force is not a real force, but rather an apparent force that arises from the car's inertia. The inertia of the car is its resistance to change in motion. When the car turns, the car's inertia wants to keep it moving in a straight line. However, the road is forcing the car to turn, so the car's inertia creates an outward force.

Centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts on a body moving in a curved path. It is directed away from the centre of the curve, and it is not a real force. Centrifugal force is caused by the inertia of the body, which resists any change in its motion. When a car takes a turn, the car's inertia wants to keep it moving in a straight line. However, the friction between the tyres and the road forces the car to turn. This causes the car to experience centrifugal force, which acts outward on the car.
Only option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. A car is moving with high velocity when it makes a turn. A force acts on it outwardly because of centrifugal force. Centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts on a body moving in a curved path. It is directed away from the centre of the curve, and it is not a real force. Centrifugal force is caused by the inertia of the body, which resists any change in its motion. When a car takes a turn, the car's inertia wants to keep it moving in a straight line. However, the friction between the tyres and the road forces the car to turn. This causes the car to experience centrifugal force, which acts outward on the car.

questions.text: A stone of mass m tied to a string of length I. It is rotated in a circle with the other end of the string as the centre. The speed of the stone is v. If the string breaks, the stone will move
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect, because the stone has a tangential velocity, which is the velocity that is tangent to the circle. When the string breaks, the only force acting on the stone is the force of inertia. This force of inertia will cause the stone to continue moving in the direction it was already moving, which is tangentially to the circle.

No, the stone will not move away from the center of the circle. The force that keeps the stone moving in a circle is centripetal force, which is provided by the tension in the string. When the string breaks, the centripetal force is no longer present, and the stone will fly off in a straight line tangent to the circle.

If the string breaks, the stone will move in a straight line tangent to the circle that it was rotating in. This is because the stone will no longer be subject to the centripetal force that was keeping it in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following equation: F = mv²/r where: F is the centripetal force (N) m is the mass of the stone (kg) v is the speed of the stone (m/s) r is the radius of the circle (m) When the string breaks, the centripetal force will be zero, and the stone will move in a straight line tangent to the circle.

questions.text: The force require to bend the normally straight path of a particle into a circular path is called_force.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The term "travelling force" is not commonly used in physics. There are a few different forces that could be considered to be "travelling forces" One example of a travelling force is the force of gravity. The force of gravity is always pulling us towards the centre of the Earth. This force is always acting on us, even when we are not moving. However, when we are moving, the force of gravity is also trying to change our direction of motion. This is why we feel a force pulling us down when we are in an aeroplane.

Centrifugal force is an apparent force that acts on a body moving in a circular path and is directed away from the centre of the circle. It is caused by the inertia of the body wanting to continue moving in a straight line.

Bending force is the internal force that is generated in a beam when it is subjected to a load that causes it to bend. The bending force is directed perpendicular to the beam's axis and is responsible for causing the beam to deform. The bending force can be calculated from the following equation: F = M/I

The force required to bend the normally straight path of a particle into a circular path is called centripetal force. Centripetal force is always directed towards the centre of the circle and is perpendicular to the particle's velocity. This force is necessary to keep the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following equation: Fc = mv²/r

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. The force required to bend the normally straight path of a particle into a circular path is called centripetal force. Centripetal force is always directed towards the centre of the circle and is perpendicular to the particle's velocity. This force is necessary to keep the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following equation: Fc = mv²/r

questions.text: The curved flight of fighter planes at high speed requires a large:

Gravitational force, frictional force, and centrifugal acceleration are not the primary forces involved in maintaining the curved flight of the planes.

questions.text: When a body is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string, the centripetal force is supplied by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It can be used to measure the centripetal force, but it does not supply centripetal force.

Yes, the centripetal force is supplied by the tension in the string. When a body is whirled in a horizontal circle by means of a string, the string exerts a force on the body towards the centre of the circle. This force is called the centripetal force. The centripetal force is necessary to keep the body moving in a circular path. The tension in the string is the force that the string exerts on the body. The tension in the string is equal to the centripetal force. The tension in the string is given by the following equation:T = Fc = mv2/r

Velocity is related to the kinetic energy and momentum of the body and is a component in the centripetal force formula, but it does not supply the force. Velocity's direction is constantly changing due to the centripetal force.
Centripetal acceleration is the result of the centripetal force acting on the mass, not the source of the force.

The tension in the string is equal to the centripetal force. The tension in the string is given by the following equation:T = Fc = mv2/r

questions.text: A particle of mass m is executing uniform circular motion on a path of radius r. If P is the magnitude of its linear momentum. The radial force acting on the particle is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option incorrectly combines mass and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.
This option incorrectly combines radius and momentum without considering the relationship with centripetal force.

The correct option. The centripetal force is related to the square of the linear momentum and inversely proportional to the radius.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. The centripetal force is given by the following formula:Fc = mv2/r where:Fc is the centripetal force (N).m is the mass of the particle (kg).v is the speed of the particle (m/s).r is the radius of the circle (m).We can write the magnitude of the linear momentum as: P = mv. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.
This option incorrectly multiplies mass, radius, and momentum without considering the correct formula for centripetal force.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.The radial force is the centripetal force that keeps the particle moving in a circular path. Substituting this into the equation for the centripetal force, we get: Fc =P2/mr. Therefore, the radial force acting on the particle is P2/mr.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they do not appropriately consider the relationship between linear momentum and centripetal force in uniform circular motion.

questions.text: A stone to the end of a string 1m is whirled ina horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 22 revolution in 44 seconds, What is the magnitude and direction of a acceleration of the stone.
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: π2/4ms-2 and direction along the radius towards the centre
options.explanation: This magnitude is incorrect. The calculation for centripetal acceleration yields π2ms-2, not π2/4ms-2.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the radius towards the centre
options.explanation: a=v²/r = ω²r = 4π²n²r = 4π² (22/44)² × 1 = π²m/s², and its direction is always along the radius and towards the centre.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the radius away from the centre
options.explanation: The magnitude is correct, but the direction is wrong. Centripetal acceleration is an "inward-seeking" force/acceleration, always directed towards the centre of the circle to maintain the circular path. It is never directed away from the centre.

options.text: π2ms-2 and direction along the tangent to the circle
options.explanation: The magnitude is correct, but the direction is wrong. Acceleration along the tangent to the circle is called tangential acceleration. Tangential acceleration occurs only when the speed of the object is changing (either increasing or decreasing). In this problem, the stone is moving with a constant speed, so the tangential acceleration is zero. The only acceleration present is the centripetal acceleration directed towards the centre.

questions.explanation: a=v²/r = ω²r = 4π²n²r = 4π² (22/44)² × 1 = π²m/s², and its direction is always along the radius and towards the centre. In uniform circular motion, the acceleration (centripetal acceleration) is always directed along the radius towards the centre of the circular path.
